content.assessment: Status quo maintenance is the politically easiest path because it requires no new coalition-building and aligns with the demonstrated veto power of church-maintained schools and middle-class families that has historically deterred structural reform even under governments with strong institutional control. However, this feasibility is a function of inertia rather than sustained legitimacy, and the minister's own framing of the admissions burden as deserving 'social consultation' suggests government credibility costs from inaction on an issue it has itself flagged as problematic.
opportunities: Monitoring data could eventually build a stronger evidentiary and political case for future reform without triggering premature opposition mobilization
strengths: No new coalition need be assembled; existing veto constituencies (church schools, middle-class families) are satisfied by default [F-live-political_feasibility-05, F-live-political_feasibility-07]

Historical precedent shows even a government with strong centralized institutional levers avoided structural reform to these tracks, indicating status quo is the politically 'safe' equilibrium [F-live-political_feasibility-07]
threats: Continued inaction while inequality indicators worsen could itself become a political liability if opposition or public opinion shifts toward blaming inertia rather than reform attempts [F-live-political_feasibility-03]
weaknesses: Monitoring without corrective action risks being read as policy avoidance, undermining the government's own stated equity rationale and legitimacy [F-live-legal_and_governance-06]
Does nothing to address the underlying inequality dynamics (170-point reading gap) that generate ongoing legitimacy pressure on the system [F-live-political_feasibility-03]
